description: Figure 1 presents a schematic representation summarizing the ordered precipitate morphologies observed in duplex low-density steels (Fe-Mn-Al-C based, with and without 5 wt.% Ni) as a function of annealing temperature. The upper Micro-scale panel depicts the grain structure where the red γ-austenite matrix contains micron-sized B2 precipitates and grain boundary B2 at higher temperatures, while blue B2 stringer bands replace the ferrite phase at 500–700 °C in the Ni-containing grade. The lower Nano-scale insets zoom into two distinct regions: the red-circle panel shows nano-sized cuboidal κ-carbides dispersed in the γ matrix (prominent at 500–700 °C in both grades), whereas the blue-circle panel illustrates nano-sized B2 particles within the α-ferrite matrix (observed at 900–1050 °C). This diagram supports the core finding that precipitate type, size, and distribution depend strongly on both temperature and Ni content, with κ-carbides dominating the lower temperature austenite and nano-B2 discs forming in ferrite at elevated temperatures.

This figure presents a multi-scale microstructural investigation of Ni-containing Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels annealed at 500–1050°C, combining scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Panels (a)–(c) are SEM micrographs at 500°C and 700°C, respectively, revealing B2 stringer bands (originating from initial α phase) within a γ + κ matrix and nano-sized κ-carbides inside γ grains. Panel (b) highlights coarse grain-boundary B2 precipitates (GB-B2) at 500°C. Panel (d) provides a higher-magnification view of the B2 stringer band and γ + κ microstructure at 700°C. Panels (e) and (f) are TEM bright-field images at the nano-scale: (e) shows nano-disk B2 precipitates in the α matrix after high-temperature annealing (900°C and 1050°C), while (f) displays cuboid κ-carbides in the γ matrix. The figure demonstrates that lower annealing temperatures (500–700°C) promote B2 stringer bands and cuboid κ-carbides in Ni-containing steel, whereas higher temperatures yield nano-disk B2 in ferrite. Caption states that Ni-free samples follow a similar trend at high temperatures but exhibit α + needle-like κ-carbides instead of B2 stringer bands at 500–700°C.

An SEM micrograph displays ordered arrays of nano-indentation impressions arranged in rows across a multi-phase microstructure of Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel. The triangular indents, produced with a Berkovich tip under a constant loading/unloading rate of 500 μN s⁻¹ to a peak load of 10 000 μN with a 20 s hold time, are distributed in a grid pattern (50–70 indentations per array) targeting distinct phases and phase mixtures. The surface exhibits contrast variations indicating different constituent phases—potentially γ-austenite, α-ferrite, B2 intermetallic bands, and κ-carbide regions—within which individual and composite hardness values were measured. The 2 μm scale bar confirms sub-micrometer to micrometer indent sizes appropriate for resolving phase-specific mechanical response. This figure provides visual evidence for the nano-indentation mapping strategy used to isolate the hardness contributions of individual phases (solution-treated γ and α, B2 stringers, micro-sized and nano-disk B2) and their combinations (e.g., γ + cuboid κ, α + needle-shape κ) as a function of annealing treatment, supporting the quantitative load-depth data reported elsewhere in the study.
